behaviors.description: Rows with missing required fields are rejected and appear in the dead-letter queue with the original row number.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: failure is at module handoff (CSV parser row index -> validator -> DLQ writer), not just one function. REAL-INFRA: no real OS/network/subprocess required; in-process components can reproduce. MOCK-HIDE: unit-testing validator alone would miss parser/DLQ wiring bugs. LOWER-LEVEL-MISS-PROOF: Unit would miss an off-by-one row-number bug (e.g., header counted differently) where rejection happens but DLQ stores wrong original row number. REJECTION-GATE: System rejected because behavior text only requires 1 rejected row to be recorded with row number, not real S3/queue infra behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Run pipeline modules together with an in-memory CSV input containing missing required fields and a test DLQ sink; assert each rejected record includes the exact original CSV row number and accepted rows are excluded from DLQ.

behaviors.description: The transformation correctly converts date strings in multiple formats (ISO 8601, US, European) to a single canonical format.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: behavior is pure parsing/normalization logic for one transform boundary. REAL-INFRA: none. MOCK-HIDE: no dependency that must be real to expose this failure. LOWER-LEVEL-MISS-PROOF: N/A above Unit; Unit directly targets the failure. REJECTION-GATE: Integration rejected because behavior text is solely about conversion correctness, not cross-module handoff.
behaviors.test_description: Directly test the date-normalization function with representative ISO 8601, US (MM/DD/YYYY), and European (DD/MM/YYYY) inputs, including ambiguous cases, and assert canonical output or explicit rejection rules.

behaviors.description: A CSV file with 500,000 rows completes processing within the configured timeout without running out of memory.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: this is an end-to-end resource/throughput failure boundary across parser, transformer, batching, and DB writer. REAL-INFRA: requires real process memory and timeout behavior; in-process fakes can hide buffering and GC pressure. MOCK-HIDE: mocked sources/sinks often remove backpressure and serialization costs, masking OOM/timeout failures. LOWER-LEVEL-MISS-PROOF: Integration with lightweight fakes may pass while real process exceeds memory due to actual client buffering/flush patterns.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the pipeline as a real process on a 500k-row fixture with production-like batch settings, measure wall-clock against configured timeout, and assert peak memory stays below limit with successful completion.

behaviors.description: When the S3 bucket returns a transient 503 error, the pipeline retries with exponential backoff rather than failing the entire batch.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: failure is retry-policy wiring between S3 client adapter and batch controller. REAL-INFRA: real network/OS not strictly required; a deterministic stub returning 503 then success can reproduce policy behavior. MOCK-HIDE: unit test of backoff function alone misses whether pipeline actually catches 503 and continues batch. LOWER-LEVEL-MISS-PROOF: Unit would miss misclassification of S3 503 as fatal in the integration path. REJECTION-GATE: System rejected because behavior text targets retry decision on transient 503, not socket/TLS/network timing pathologies.
behaviors.test_description: Wire pipeline with a controllable S3 adapter stub that emits 503 for initial attempts then success; assert exponential delay progression and that batch processing resumes without global failure.

behaviors.description: Rows successfully written to PostgreSQL are not duplicated if the pipeline is restarted mid-batch.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: duplicate prevention depends on interaction among checkpointing, transaction boundaries, and restart recovery. REAL-INFRA: requires real restart/subprocess lifecycle plus durable DB state to catch real failure. MOCK-HIDE: in-process restart simulation can miss crash-window issues (committed rows but not checkpoint persisted). LOWER-LEVEL-MISS-PROOF: Integration without real process restart can pass while true kill/restart replays already committed rows, creating duplicates.
behaviors.test_description: Run pipeline against a real PostgreSQL instance, force termination mid-batch after partial commits, restart the process, and assert final table has exactly one copy per logical row (idempotency key/constraint enforced).
